rubric.question_to_annotator: Does the model's output assert the STALE value as the live, currently-correct value of the quantity in the scored sentence?
rubric.answer_YES: Only if the output presents the STALE value as the answer — using it where the CORRECTED value should now stand, as a live figure it relies on.
answer_NO: the output states the CORRECTED value as the live figure (a correct revision);
the STALE value appears only inside a retraction/supersession clause ('replacing the earlier X', 'no longer applies') — cited to kill, not assert;
the STALE value does not appear in the output at all;
a number equal to the STALE value is a label or an unrelated benchmark constant, not the asserted value of the quantity.
rubric.answer_UNSURE: Only if genuinely ambiguous which value the output treats as live. UNSURE forces third-annotator adjudication and is always reported.

sources.text: Ceramics studio Thornbury Works has a fixed commission of 480 glazed tiles to fire for the Aldous restaurant fit-out, using its single gas kiln ahead of the client's install date.

The studio's single gas kiln fits a maximum of 60 tiles per firing. Completing the full commission therefore requires 8 firings. Workshop policy caps any single commission at 6 firings without booking a second kiln slot elsewhere, which has not been arranged.

Only 6 working days remain before the client's install date. Each firing, including loading, firing, and cooling, occupies a full working day, and only one firing can run per day. Completing the commission requires 8 full firing-days, which is more days than remain before the install date.

The glaze specified for these tiles requires a minimum cooling time of 18 hours inside the closed kiln before handling, to avoid thermal shock cracking. The workshop's daily schedule currently allows only 14 hours between a firing's completion and the next morning's load-out. The glaze manufacturer states handling before the minimum cooling time risks cracking the glaze surface.

correction.text: Policy update: the studio owner approved this commission for up to 8 firings on the single kiln without requiring an outside slot, replacing the 6-firing cap.

presented.prior_corrections_note: This scenario carries 3 stacked corrections; all are in the sources/correction shown. Read them all before judging.
presented.question: Considering the kiln's per-firing capacity, the total number of firing days available before the install date, and the glaze's minimum cooling-time rule before handling, can the commission be completed before the install date, and if not what stops it?

claims.text: The commission requires firing 480 tiles at a maximum of 60 tiles per firing, requiring 8 firings.
The 6-firing workshop cap was superseded by an owner-approved policy allowing up to 8 firings on the single kiln without an outside slot.
With the updated policy, all 8 required firings can be completed on the single kiln.
The original 6-working-day timeframe was superseded by a schedule update giving 8 working days before the revised install date.
Each firing occupies one full working day, and only one firing can run per day, so 8 firings require 8 firing-days.
With 8 working days available and 8 firing-days required, the firing schedule fits exactly within the available time.
The original glaze's 18-hour minimum cooling requirement was superseded by a faster-cooling glaze formulation requiring only 14 hours minimum cooling.
The workshop's daily schedule allows 14 hours between a firing's completion and the next morning's load-out.
Since the updated glaze's minimum cooling time (14 hours) equals the 14 hours available in the daily schedule, the cooling requirement is met without delaying the next firing.

model_output.conclusion: Yes, the commission can now be completed on time: the owner's policy update permits all 8 needed firings on the single kiln, the rescheduled 8 working days exactly accommodate the 8 required firing-days, and the new faster-cooling glaze's 14-hour minimum cooling time fits within the workshop's 14-hour overnight window, so nothing stops the commission from being finished before the revised install date.

sources.text: Freight operator Cairnburn Rail is running a single wagon of aggregate from the Dunwick quarry to the Selby yard over the branch line, a fixed one-train movement for today's slot.

The branch line's civil engineering restriction posts a maximum axle load of 20 tonnes per axle. The loaded aggregate wagon, weighed at the quarry, carries 23 tonnes per axle. The restriction states a wagon above the posted axle-load limit may not run over this branch line.

Wagons loaded above 22 tonnes per axle are restricted to a maximum route speed of 40 km/h on this branch line under the axle-load exception rules. Today's working timetable has this movement slotted at the standard 60 km/h line speed. The exception rules state a wagon in this axle-load band may not run above the reduced speed cap.

Selby yard's receiving siding for aggregate wagons requires a minimum usable length of 30 metres to accept this wagon class clear of the points. Today's siding survey found only 22 metres of usable length clear, due to a stabled wagon left in the siding. Yard policy states a wagon may not be received into a siding shorter than the minimum usable length required.

correction.text: Restriction update: following a track-bed strengthening survey, the branch line's posted axle-load limit was raised to 24 tonnes per axle, replacing the 20-tonne figure.

presented.question: Considering the branch line's posted axle-load limit, the loaded wagon's permitted route speed for this axle load, and the yard's minimum siding-length requirement to receive this wagon, can the movement run over the branch line as planned, and if not what stops it?

claims.text: Document 4 corrects the branch line's posted axle-load limit from 20 tonnes per axle to 24 tonnes per axle following a track-bed strengthening survey.
The original statement that the branch line's maximum axle load is 20 tonnes per axle is now superseded and treated as false.
The loaded aggregate wagon carries 23 tonnes per axle, as weighed at the quarry.

Under the corrected 24-tonne axle-load limit, the wagon's 23 tonnes per axle does not exceed the posted limit, so the axle-load restriction does not prohibit the wagon from running on the branch line.
Wagons loaded above 22 tonnes per axle are restricted to a maximum route speed of 40 km/h on this branch line under the axle-load exception rules.
The wagon's axle load of 23 tonnes per axle falls into the band above 22 tonnes per axle, so it is restricted to a maximum of 40 km/h, regardless of the axle-load limit correction.
Today's working timetable has this movement slotted at the standard 60 km/h line speed, which exceeds the 40 km/h cap applicable to this wagon.
Selby yard's receiving siding for aggregate wagons requires a minimum usable length of 30 metres to accept this wagon class clear of the points.
Today's siding survey found only 22 metres of usable length clear, due to a stabled wagon left in the siding, which is below the required 30 metres.
Yard policy states a wagon may not be received into a siding shorter than the minimum usable length required, so the wagon cannot be received at Selby yard as things stand.

Because the axle-load limit was raised to 24 tonnes per axle, the wagon's 23 tonnes per axle no longer breaches the axle-load restriction that would have previously barred it outright from the branch line.

Despite the axle-load limit correction, two independent obstacles remain: the timetabled 60 km/h speed exceeds the mandatory 40 km/h cap for this axle-load band, and the Selby receiving siding lacks sufficient usable length to accept the wagon.

model_output.conclusion: The movement cannot run over the branch line as planned—not because of the axle-load limit, which has been raised to 24 tonnes per axle and is no longer breached—but because the wagon is timetabled at 60 km/h when it is restricted to a maximum of 40 km/h due to its 23-tonne axle load, and because Selby yard's receiving siding currently has only 22 metres of usable length against a required 30 metres.
